title: Vasculitis: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Identify key diagnostic features of Vasculitis
Apply clinical reasoning to Vasculitis management

sections.title: Overview of Vasculitis
sections.content:
**Vasculitis**



**Key Points:**
• Large vessel: Giant cell arteritis (>50 years, temporal headache, jaw claudication, vision loss emergency) and Takayasu (young Asian women, aortic arch)
• Medium vessel: Polyarteritis nodosa (hepatitis B, renal/GI/skin, NO lung involvement, aneurysms on angiography)
• Small vessel ANCA+: GPA (Wegener) = c-ANCA/PR3, upper/lower airway + kidney; MPA = p-ANCA/MPO, kidney + lung

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• Large vessel: Giant cell arteritis (>50 years, temporal headache, jaw claudication, vision loss emergency) and Takayasu (young Asian women, aortic arch)
• Medium vessel: Polyarteritis nodosa (hepatitis B, renal/GI/skin, NO lung involvement, aneurysms on angiography)

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Small vessel ANCA+: GPA (Wegener) = c-ANCA/PR3, upper/lower airway + kidney; MPA = p-ANCA/MPO, kidney + lung
• Small vessel immune complex: IgA vasculitis (HSP) = palpable purpura + arthritis + abdominal pain + renal (children post-URI)
• GCA emergency: temporal artery biopsy is gold standard BUT start high-dose steroids IMMEDIATELY (don't wait for biopsy to prevent blindness)

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Vasculitis:**

• Small vessel ANCA+: GPA (Wegener) = c-ANCA/PR3, upper/lower airway + kidney; MPA = p-ANCA/MPO, kidney + lung
• Small vessel immune complex: IgA vasculitis (HSP) = palpable purpura + arthritis + abdominal pain + renal (children post-URI)
• GCA emergency: temporal artery biopsy is gold standard BUT start high-dose steroids IMMEDIATELY (don't wait for biopsy to prevent blindness)

mcqs.question: Which of the following is MOST characteristic of Vasculitis?

options.text: Large vessel: Giant cell arteritis (>50 years, temporal headache, jaw claudication, vision loss emergency) and Takayasu (young Asian women, aortic arc
options.explanation: Large vessel: Giant cell arteritis (>50 years, temporal headache, jaw claudication, vision loss emergency) and Takayasu (young Asian women, aortic arch)

options.text: Requires invasive testing in all cases

mcqs.question: What is the KEY diagnostic or management consideration for Vasculitis?

options.text: GCA emergency: temporal artery biopsy is gold standard BUT start high-dose steroids IMMEDIATELY (don't wait for biopsy to prevent blindness)
options.explanation: GCA emergency: temporal artery biopsy is gold standard BUT start high-dose steroids IMMEDIATELY (don't wait for biopsy to prevent blindness)

options.text: Immediate surgical intervention in all cases

mcqs.question: A patient presents with findings consistent with Vasculitis. Which finding would you expect?

options.text: Medium vessel: Polyarteritis nodosa (hepatitis B, renal/GI/skin, NO lung involvement, aneurysms on angiography)
options.explanation: Medium vessel: Polyarteritis nodosa (hepatitis B, renal/GI/skin, NO lung involvement, aneurysms on angiography)

mcqs.stem: A 65-year-old woman with new-onset headache, jaw claudication, and ESR of 85 mm/hr is suspected of having giant cell arteritis. What is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Start high-dose prednisone (1 mg/kg/day) immediately, then arrange temporal artery biopsy
options.explanation: Per ACR/EULAR guidelines, GCA with visual symptoms is an emergency. High-dose glucocorticoids must be started IMMEDIATELY to prevent irreversible vision loss. Temporal artery biopsy remains diagnostic even after several weeks of steroid therapy due to persistent inflammatory changes.

options.text: Schedule temporal artery biopsy first to confirm diagnosis before starting treatment
options.explanation: Waiting for biopsy before treatment risks permanent vision loss. Biopsy can be performed within 2 weeks of starting steroids without significantly affecting sensitivity.

options.text: Start low-dose prednisone (10 mg/day) and monitor symptoms for 1 week
options.explanation: Low-dose steroids are insufficient for GCA. The recommended dose is 40-60 mg/day for uncomplicated GCA, or 1 mg/kg/day (up to IV pulse methylprednisolone 1g × 3 days) when visual symptoms are present.

options.text: Order CRP and IL-6 levels before making treatment decisions
options.explanation: While CRP and IL-6 are elevated in GCA, additional testing should not delay treatment in a patient with classic symptoms and elevated ESR. Vision loss can occur within hours.
